text: After Saul died, David came back from defeating the Amalekites and stayed two days at Ziklag.
On the third day a man came from Saul's camp with his clothes torn and dirt on his head; he came before David and bowed to the ground.
David asked where he had come from, and he answered that he had escaped from Israel's camp.
David asked how the battle had gone, and the man said the army had fled, many had fallen, and Saul and his son Jonathan were dead.
The young man said he happened to be on Mount Gilboa and saw Saul leaning on his spear with the enemy chariots closing in.
Saul turned, saw him, and asked who he was; he answered that he was an Amalekite.
Saul asked him to stand over him and kill him, since he was in anguish but still alive.
So he stood over Saul and killed him, since he knew Saul could not survive his wound; then he took the crown on his head and the armlet on his arm and brought them to David.
David tore his clothes, and so did all the men with him.
They mourned and wept and fasted until evening for Saul, for Jonathan, and for the people of the LORD and the house of Israel who had fallen by the sword.
David asked the young man where he was from, and he answered that he was the son of a foreigner living among them, an Amalekite.
David asked how he was not afraid to raise his hand to destroy the LORD's anointed.
David called one of his men and told him to strike the Amalekite down, and he struck him so that he died.
David said his own mouth had testified against him, saying 'I killed the LORD's anointed.'
David sang this lament over Saul and his son Jonathan.
He ordered it taught to the people of Judah; it is called the Song of the Bow, and it is written in the Book of Jashar.
Your glory, Israel, lies slain on your heights; how the mighty have fallen!
Do not tell it in Gath or the streets of Ashkelon, or the daughters of the Philistines will rejoice.
Mountains of Gilboa, let no dew or rain fall on you, for there the shield of the mighty was defiled, the shield of Saul, no longer rubbed with oil.
From the blood of the slain, from the flesh of the mighty, Jonathan's bow did not turn back, and Saul's sword did not return empty.
Saul and Jonathan, loved and gracious in life, were not divided in death; swifter than eagles, stronger than lions.
Daughters of Israel, weep for Saul, who clothed you in scarlet and finery and put gold ornaments on your dresses.
How the mighty have fallen in the midst of battle; Jonathan lies slain on your heights.
David grieved for Jonathan, his brother, whose love was more wonderful to him than the love of women.
How the mighty have fallen, and the weapons of war have perished.
